sentences.text: Yes.
Every rabbi, who is-- he's a Talmudical scholar.
The-- they form a-- a kehilla in the shtetl.
They pick up the wisest men, the rabbi, the shochet, sometimes a-- a mohel, you know.
All these, you know, these men, they are active.
For example, my father specially, he was very active.
We had a rabbi who was a very young man.
So the people had more confidence in my father, who was a little older.
For example, the Jewish people, we didn't-- we didn't have a lawyer in our shtetl.
So it was a conflict about, yeah, but we young people when they needed a divorce, of course, they came to my father for advice what to do.
Perhaps they could get together, you know-- he should straighten them out.
What the reason of the divorce was?
Has to be a reason.
Jewish divorce wasn't easy.
And [INAUDIBLE], like my father, he was a rabbi and very educated.
So he will take them to the-- to the other room, which we call it the [INAUDIBLE].
And he will sit down with the young couple and explain them about the divorce and try, you know, to-- you know, try they should get together and so and so.
Many times, my father used to tell me to get out.
Because he was afraid that-- I was a girl at about age 10.
I shouldn't gossip about this young couple.
He said, Rachela, please.
[NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE] I make believe I'm leaving.
But I was so sneaky that sometimes I put my ear to the door.
And I wanted to listen what this young girl is talking about her husband.
At the age of 10, I was [LAUGHS] so annoying.
He was-- also, he took-- like, if somebody-- you know, the [INAUDIBLE], he didn't take care.
Because we had special people for that.
But the most important, there was no welfare.
There were many poor people in the shtetl.
When it came on a Passover, they didn't have no money to conduct a Pesach, not to buy matzohs, not to buy wine.
The main thing, it was matzohs and wine and potatoes, what these poor people needed for Passover, you know?
The rest, they could manage.
But this was the main course what a Jewish family was buying.
Matzohs, potatoes-- you know? --eggs, onions, carrots.
That's all what you needed for Passover.
From that, you could make anything.
From the matzohs, we could make, uh-- you know, you know, we used to make them crumbled up and bake cake and from eggs, you know, with the matzoh meal.
And many, many dishes for Passover we could make from matzohs, of course.
And meat was the main course, of course.
But the rich people, if they had the chicken, one was enough.
But where to take the money, that was another thing.
You could buy everything, like I mentioned before.
But the money, you could not buy it.
There were people, very decent people, very shy.
Nobody would know what it's doing in their home.
They were so-- they lived with dignity-- with shame.
But my father would know who doesn't-- with the other-- he had a partner-- also a shochet-- there were two-- two shochetim-- two, you know, ritual slaughterers in our city.
These two men would know who doesn't have.
They will walk from before Passover, like a month before Passover.
They will walk from house to house and knock at the door-- [KNOCKING OFF-CAMERA] [INAUDIBLE] --a knock at the door and say, we need charity.
They would not say-- they would not tell them for whom.
Because I used to listen when my-- he used to tell my mother.
We didn't tell.
He only told my mother.
But he would not tell nobody.
He just begged for money.
Give me a quarter, give me $0.10, give me $0.05.
If somebody gave $1-- a zloty-- $1, that was a lot of money.
But I was so sneaky all the time, I'd just listen when my father was telling to my mother, you know, how much money.
They came in.
They closed the door.
They used to count the money and make rolls, you know, and then go to their house and give them the money to buy the food for their family.
That was his mitzvah.
That was charity.
This was the welfare from our people.
And this was the responsibility for my father to do that.
You know?
And with the other men, partners, they did that.
He was also-- if somebody didn't get along in the business, there was always fights.
Like, one was-- borrowed money, he didn't want to give them back and the business-- so, so, so-- they always-- our house was always filled with those people.
Always used to come in-- Mr.
Warszauer, he owes me so much, so much, so much.
Come in to the [INAUDIBLE].
Sit down.
Take it easy.
My father calmed him down.
Don't fight.
No argument.
He told them, according to the law, what he should do to give them back or pay them half or pay them every once in six months, and so and so.
But this was my father.
He was always busy.

Not just doing his job, by doing, you know, his job, but always busy endlessly taking care of people, thinking about if somebody got sick, you know, giving him a visit and if the [INAUDIBLE] was taking place according to the orthodox way.
If a special man or woman died who was very orthodox-- special orthodox-- they gave him special privilege, these men, to do according to the-- to the Jewish law, you know, not to fail with anything.
So my father was busy with those things.
And, of course, my mother was busy.
On the other hand, she was busy in her way.
She was busy crocheting, doing cooking, washing the dishes, [? knitting ?,] cleaning.
Like I say, both of them kept busy.
And if it was an evening in the winter, when it was cold-- like, you know, we didn't go no place.
My father would sit down and play [INAUDIBLE] with his partner, the other shochet.
And he will enjoy that.
I would walk over to the table.
And I would say, Daddy, put the king here or put the queen here.
And he would say, don't tell me what to do!
Look-- she's learning already the [INAUDIBLE].
When I was a little girl, I used to love it-- the way they were moving the kings and the queens, this and that.
He was always busy, and because-- and besides, spending time [NON-ENGLISH], learning.
This was the most ritual thing for Hasidic people to do.
If I'm Hasidic boys, they spent hours learning in the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, for a couple hours.
That kept them busy, too.
Yeah.
Yeah.
My older sister Tobcha, she was a beautiful girl.
Not because I'm saying she was beautiful, but my parents thought that she's really beautiful.
She was blonde with blue eyes, blonde hair, and a beautiful figure.
Well, she was married when she was 17.
A very rich fellow picked her up.
He was in the lumber yard business, very well-provided.
His name was Izrael Blizinksi.
And he met her on the train while my sister went to visit grandfather in Skarzysko.
My grandfather lived in Skarzysko those years.
And he met her, and he asked her for her name.
And he sent a [INAUDIBLE].
He didn't care for anything.
But he was-- he was, at the time, 29.
And my sister was 17.
My mother, father was-- they want-- the only thing they weren't so happy, because of the age a little bit.
And they wanted her-- they thought-- they want to enjoy her for another two years to be home.
But most of the young girls they married-- in the Hasidic homes, they married in that age-- 17, 16, 17, 18.
By 20, they thought they're old already, you know?
So she was picked up really very fast.
And she was married.
She lived in the city of Szydlowiec.
And she had two little girls.
The older one was Rachel.
She carried after the same grandmother, the way I carried my name.
And the younger one was Chajale.
Once in a while, for the holidays, she used to come home with her grandchildren.
We were all so happy to see them.
And me, as a young girl, I was, you know, in that time I was 8, 9, 10.
I used to love to play with my nieces.
I loved to.
My nieces were the world to me.
I spent hours and playing.
Because, as a young girl, I used to-- I-- I love children.
I love children.
I love animals.
I love birds.
I love nature.
I love sceneries.
But special, children-- who doesn't love children?
And special my nieces.
That wouldn't be normal to say that I wouldn't love them.
But I used to spend hours in playing with them as a girl.
But she came just home for a while, for the holidays, sometimes, not every time.
Because our apartment was small.
And to come home with the family was, you know, a little bit too-- not convenient.
So she tried, most of the time, to stay home.
But here and then they came home.
Because my parents used to be so delighted.
All of us were delighted to see them.
My sister Esther, who had a nickname in Poland.
In my town, they used to call her Jadwiga Smosarksa.
Jadwiga Smosarska was a movie actress in Warsaw.
She was known like Elizabeth Taylor, in America.
Beautiful woman.
My sister had green eyes with olive skin.
She was the opposite from my sister, Esther, from my sister Tobcha.
She was really with chestnut-- beautiful chestnut hair.
She was really a beauty.
Well, my mother thought she had a hard time, really, when she was in school.
All the Gentile boys used to kibitz her.
They used to, you know, stop her and telling her, why she is so religious?
Why she is not friendly with the non-Jewish boys?
But, of course, it was a custom.
And she was brought up, you know?
She smiled back.
And she came home.
She thanked them for the compliment.
They used to tell her, in Polish, how beautiful she was.
[SPEAKING POLISH] That's all what I heard from her what she was telling my mother.
She came back from school.
She says, this one, this non-Jewish fellow stopped her.
This non-Jewish-- my father-- my mother used to say, please, don't tell this to my father-- to your father.
Because your father wouldn't let you go to school.
He would be afraid.
But try to-- to-- to walk briskly home, to avoid these boys.
And also the same coincident that a fellow came to our city.
And he saw my sister walking.
And he-- he came to visit his aunt.
And he says, who is this girl?
Eh, this is Warszauer daughter, the younger one.
And he went home.
And he sent a [INAUDIBLE] again.
This fellow, his name was Josef, Nuger second name.
They lived in Suchedniow.
And soon after, they send a [INAUDIBLE] again.
And my mother says, oh, my god, my daughter's leaving me so fast.
I-- I-- I didn't enjoy them growing up, really.
And they're taking them out from the house.
But my father-- all the neighbors envied her.
Look, Mrs.
Warszauer, you are blessed to have beautiful children.
That's a blessing.
You don't need to have money.
You have good [NON-ENGLISH].
You have good [NON-ENGLISH].
[INAUDIBLE] is, you know, merchandise.
You sell it fast.
So that was it.
She was married to him.
And she moved away to Suchedniow.
And the parents had a [INAUDIBLE] store, you know.
And my brother-in-law was with them in the business.
And my sister went in right away to a home, which was good, you know?
Everything was provided for her.
And she had two little boys.
One, before-- when I met my sister, it was one was two, two years old, a year and a half, you know.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was pregnant, I think, with the second one.
And that was-- I remember, you know, [INAUDIBLE] came home with her child, still looking beautiful.
And she didn't wear a wig, you know?
According-- Jewish, young-- young and old, they were wearing wigs.
She was wearing a turban.
She didn't want to wear the wig.
She had such beautiful hair.
She came into the house, she took off the wig.
So did my older sister, Tobcha.
When they came home to visit their parents, they didn't want to, you know, bring-- you know, didn't want to hurt the parents that they didn't believe too much in wigs.
They wanted to be a little more modern.
Because their husbands weren't-- they were Hasidic husbands.
Both of them were Hasids, but not fanatics.
They don't have payots.
They were more on the modern side.
He was-- Tobcha's husband was a Mizrahi.
And Josef had his rabbinical-- he was a rabbi.
He had his law in-- in rabbinical.
But he didn't practice, because his parents wanted him to be in the business.
But he was very educated in Jewish law, but modern.
But her-- their husbands were not against it they-- if they wore turbans, or that-- you know, they didn't pay-- before the war it was-- the young people started to be more modern.
And a lot of them didn't wear wigs, even from Hasidic homes.
But my mother did, of course.
She had to.
And she had to wear the wig every time when she walked out.
Even myself, she wouldn't let me wear short sleeves.
But going to school, I used to rebel when I was a girl.
I remember.
I used to have a special two dresses made with short sleeves, because I could not wear long sleeves.
Every girl had short sleeves.
And I was crying to my mother.
And I says, ma, I want to wear short sleeves.
It's so hot.
It's in the summer.
They're all wearing shorts sleeves.
I don't want to be different than all the girls.
I'm not talking about the gentiles, about the Jewish girls.
And my mother says, [NON-ENGLISH], soon, when you come home, take off that dress and put it in the closet that Papa shouldn't see it.
So I did.
But I was always sneaky.
I always wanted to be on the modern side, because it pull me more to the modern side, always.
And-- and that's what I did.
And my mother used to give me a smile.
She says, OK, do it.
But it was a gossip going around in our shtetl.
Because gossip was very, very-- it was gossip in the shtetl.
Because one lived near each other.
You could hear every knock from each other.
And if somebody walked on the street, you know, they would tell how she walked and how she dressed.
And everybody was gossiping about each other.
Most of the people, the Jewish people, they did that in the smaller shtetls.
In our house, we didn't gossip, because this was a sin, to gossip.
My mother will say, sh!
Don't ever talk about nobody.
My father, [INAUDIBLE], that was the biggest sin.
You could never say that.
But most of the people, you know, in the town, they would gossip.
So they will talk to the rabbi, to our rabbi in shtetl.
And they will tell him that Solomon Szlomo's younger girl is wearing short sleeves to school.
Because then-- their daughters came home from school.
And they told their parents, if Solomon's daughter can wear short sleeves, why can't I?
So the rabbi says to my father, you see?
You're spoiling the young-- the young children.
The young girls will get spoiled, because of your daughter.
If she's wearing short sleeves, they will take that advice, you know?
So my father used to tell my mother, don't let her go with that dress to school.
So my mother used to tell me, Rachela, [NON-ENGLISH].
Please, you have to obey the way other kids are.
And not just other kids, you have to give a good example for other kids.
You cannot do it.
I thought that nobody will see and nobody will tell.
But when they brought up that, you cannot do it.
Then forget about it, I says.
It's not so important.
So I went-- I wanted to please my parents.
That was more important then-- and I had to-- and wear the long sleeves.
And my dresses with the short sleeves were hanging.
And I never wore them again.
That was the end.
My oldest brother, Zisman, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was a Talmudical scholar.
They all were Talmudical scholars, except the youngest that didn't have a chance to learn so much, because of interrupting of the war.
But he went to yeshiva.
He learned at Warsaw Yeshiva and the-- you know, and the Bronowice Yeshiva.
They went from one yeshiva to the other.
You know, and then before the war started, of course, they all came home.
They didn't learn too much.
They learned for a couple of years.
When they were young teenagers, they learned in the synagogue.
They learned in the synagogue with all the-- all the students.
My-- my younger brother, Leibl, he was two years younger than my brother Zisman.
He was a scholar-- a very, very, very highly educated.
He learned in the Lublin Yeshiva, which my parents were very proud of him.
He was good-looking.
He had also blue eyes, blond hair.
He looked more Irish.
And I always look forward for the holidays he should come home.
He was-- he didn't wear payots at the Lublin Yeshiva.
He was wearing more like in a suit-- in a dark suit.
You know?
But little payot around under his ears that we couldn't see it.
And he-- he studied at the end before the war, when he came home, you know.
He studied so much that my father sometimes was afraid that he is bending down, that he's changing on the other side talking about the communism, talking about the Trotsky, about Lenin.
He read about them.
And my father, you know, couldn't understand to send him to the Lublin Yeshiva, and coming home he's starting to talk about them.
He says, why do you tell-- and he brought up the whole system and everything.
I heard them.
Sometimes my father quarrelled with him.
He didn't like that idea.
But, of course, he wanted to please my parents.
He-- he stopped.
He didn't talk.
As a matter of fact, to please his parents, he will take the Talmud and go to the [INAUDIBLE] and sit and learn for a couple of hours and come home with a smile.
If he learned there or not, his mind was in a different direction, we don't know about that.
But to please the parents, respect, and koved, that was the most important at home.
Koved means respect.
Next brother was Berl.
Berl was one who had-- he was-- he-- when he was a child, we noticed that his speech wasn't correct.
He was not clear.
So my parents decided-- when he was 14 years old, it was very difficult for him to learn in a public school, too.
He went to public school.
He knew the math very well and right.
But the teacher didn't bother him, like calling over to say a poem or read loud for the class.
Because he knew that when he will start talking he will get nervous.
And he will-- wouldn't be able to bring out clear the words.
So he let him go.
And my parents decided that he should learn a profession.
He was the only one in the family, I will say.
Of course, that he should become a match-- a watch-- to prepare watches-- to work on watches, you know, to--
Yes, right.
So we send him to Starachowice.
And over there, he was learning.
It's a matter of fact that this was the most wonderful profession.
When the Germans came in, you know, that was a big help for him.
And then my next brother, that was my young-- then I came.
Of course, I wanted to be-- I was dreaming, you know.
If not the war, I wanted to become a Hebrew teacher.
That's what I wanted, to teach other children Hebrew.
And my goal was for that.
But out-- our shtetl didn't have no schools.
That school was in Krakow.
So my parents says, if you get a year older, you will-- you will be about 15, 15 and a half, 16.
We will send you to Krakow.
And you will attend the seminary, seminary from Hebrew seminary.
And so that's what happened.
But, you know, that was-- I was interrupted.
The war broke out.
That was just dreams and that's all.
When the war broke out, I was 15.
What happened when the war broke out?
How the war broke out--
How the war broke out-- I'll tell you how the war broke out.
A few months before, it was already rumors that-- I stayed with my sister in Suchedniow.
About two months before the war broke out, I was there.
Esther, in Suchedniow.
I was on vacation, my last vacation from my public school.
I graduated.
So I went to her.
I stayed there for two weeks.
My parents allowed me to come home very fast.
She allowed my brothers to come home from-- my brother Leibl to come home as soon as he can.
She wanted her family to be together, because they were talking about the war.
She remembered the First World War-- how it was.
At that time, in the First World War, she-- my mother was pregnant.
And she went through a lot.
They used to drag my father all the time to go to the army.
It was so horrible.

She used to tell us-- every Friday night at the dinner, at the table, we used to talk about-- my mother used to bring out at the time that her children should never have to go through what they went through.
It was so bad, what a war means.
But as children, I listened to my mother.
I didn't pay too much attention.
To me, it looks like our children listen to me.
I was sensitive, but I didn't cry.
I felt bad, but I didn't take it serious.
I was still busy with books.
And-- and I was dreaming I will attend school in Krakow.
I was busy with my girlfriends.
But meantime, boys used to come in.
They used to say they're taking them to the Polish army.
They said goodbye to my parents.
They wanted the blessing from my parents, from my father specially.
And my mother allowed me to come home from Suchedniow from my sister, because they're talking about the war.
Everybody was talking about the war, war, war.
But, to me, war, war, war-- it seemed like, a-- like war.
I knew it was horrible, the way they sounded.
But then we heard about the Crystal Night what was doing in Germany.
How my father used to-- my mother used to cry for burning the Jewish law with the-- with the-- with the Hebrew-- with the, you know, Talmudic law what they were burning.
My father-- oh, god!
He says, the world is all still.
Nobody is stopping them!
What will happen?
What will happen?
It was so much fear for the people, especially for the Jewish people.
Because-- only for us.
Because we were talking between us.
Young men were [? taken ?] to the Polish army.
But this was just so fast.
When I left my sister, she took me to the train with my brother-in-law Josef.
She was holding her child on her arm.
I didn't think then that was-- this would be my last good bye to them and my last kiss in love to that little boy-- to that little child-- that I would not see them anymore.
Would I think about it?
But I left them.
We're going to stop now.
